# Transcrição: 15-Rapid Fire Testing

- **Fonte:** 15-Rapid Fire Testing
- **Duração:** 04:52
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:05]

Okay, so let's talk about rapid fire testing. Now this is a methodology that we use inside of the agency and we started using it. We came up with it about a year ago and we just discovered that it worked so well before we even like went straight into driving traffic into campaigns, into website story. We were running rapid fire tests to validate the audiences, validate the offer and validate the creatives. So here's kind of how it works. Now we're going to set up rapid fire tests as a post engagement objective campaign. So all our rapid fire testing is going to be run as a PPE objective campaign. Now you might be asking why? Why don't I set it up as a conversion based objective? You can. I'm not saying you can't, but here's the thing. If you went through the training around thinking like the algorithm, which I highly recommend you go back and refresh your memory with that one at least once a month, you'll know that the three things that come into the auction and determine how well you're going to do on Facebook comes down to your bid, expected actions and then the relevance and quality of what you're putting out there. So when you're running a conversion objective campaign, however, there's another factor that comes into the relevance and quality and that is what we call the CVR, so the conversion rate. And Facebook basically calculates, let's say you're optimizing for purchases at your ad set, Facebook will calculate what is the conversion rate, how many people are converting and anything under 8% isn't going to do too well in Facebook's eyes. So Facebook starts to go, well, listen, your campaigns are not converting that well. So I'm going to actually show this audience a different creative from a competitor because they're doing a lot better than you are. So you basically get downgraded in the auction process. And oftentimes this will show up as higher CPMs. Sometimes it's stalling. So you can't even spend money at this stage. When we set it up as a post engagement objective, however, we're using a different part of the algorithm. The CVR does not come into play here. And so we get a lot clearer picture without stalling. We're basically removing this variable from the equation and potentially hindering our results. So here's the sequence of how we rapid fire test. The first stage is we test out our audiences. Now usually this is look-alikes, behavioral, interest-based, demographics. So we're trying to find cold audiences that we can go after that will continue to feed our funnels. The second stage, once we've identified audiences that look promising, and when we're running

## [03:05 - 04:52]

these campaigns, guys, just FYI, we're not necessarily expecting sales, although that would be great. What we're looking at is the responsiveness of the audience. Do we have really good soft stats front end, so good CPC, a good click-through rate? That's really what we're looking for there. The second stage is the offer. So you might be testing out different personas or categories on the site or even different products. So maybe they don't know what their best seller is because they're brand new, or they have been running everything to one product, but they want to scale growing another product. So you're testing out different products and offers here. And then the third stage is when you've identified the offer and the audiences that are working really well. Well, here is where you basically try to drive down the cost of those results even more by testing out different creatives, so your copy and your visuals. Although just to bear in mind, we will be testing out at least 10 ad creatives even in stage one. All right, now if you're looking at spend, roughly this is how much you should allocate for each stage in the testing phase. So you can see it's possible to spend up to $2,000 just on testing. And in fact, if you're onboarding clients in the e-commerce space properly, and they're especially a brand new client, that's exactly what we would tell them. We would say the first month is testing, and you should allocate between $1,000 and $2,000 to that. But if you're working towards the lower end of things, you can kind of get away with sort of $700, $500 budget. You'd be really squeezing it at that $500 stage. So just be aware of that.
